title: Kundalini and Hope: Finding It When You're in the Darkest Part of the Process

body:
Kundalini and Hope: Finding It When You're in the Darkest Part of the Process

You're here, aren't you? Probably scrolling on your phone at 3 AM, heart pounding, mind racing. You're feeling things you can't explain, seeing things that aren't there, or maybe everything just feels like it's falling apart. You're terrified. You're alone. I know that feeling. I've been there. That deep, cold dread that clamps down on your chest, making it hard to breathe, hard to believe anything will ever be normal again. You're wondering if you're losing your mind, if you're broken beyond repair. And some part of you, a small, desperate flicker, is whispering "kundalini" - but even that word just adds to the terror, doesn't it? It sounds so powerful, so uncontrollable. So yeah, I get it.

Let's get one thing straight right off the bat: you're not crazy. You're not. What you're experiencing, as utterly bizarre and terrifying as it feels, has a name. It has a history. Other people have been through it. And they've come out the other side. That's what I want you to hold onto right now, even if it feels impossible. You're not alone in this dark space. Not really. I'm here, and I've walked this path. It's a brutal path, honestly. It's not some fluffy spiritual journey with incense and chanting. It's a raw, visceral stripping away of everything you thought you were. And it hurts like hell. But there's hope. There really is.

The Despair of the Dark Night

You're probably in what they call the "dark night of the soul," or maybe even something more intense, a "spiritual emergency." You know that feeling, right? Like the bottom has dropped out of your world. Everything that used to define you - your job, your relationships, your hobbies, even your sense of self - it all feels meaningless, or worse, like it's actively attacking you. Your body might be doing weird things: tremors, intense heat or cold, strange sensations, inexplicable pains. Your mind is a whirlwind of fear, confusion, anxiety, maybe even visions or voices. Sleep is a distant memory, or when you do sleep, the dreams are nightmares. You're exhausted, utterly spent, but your system won't shut down. It's a constant, relentless assault. Know what I mean?

I remember lying awake for days, feeling like electricity was coursing through my veins, my brain buzzing with thoughts that weren't mine, images flashing behind my eyelids. I thought for sure I was having a psychotic break. The doctors didn't get it. They just wanted to medicate me into oblivion, and that scared me even more. I felt like I was losing my identity, piece by agonizing piece. It's an ego death, you see. And your ego, bless its heart, it doesn't want to die. It fights back with everything it's got, and that fight feels like pure agony inside you. It's why you feel so much resistance, so much fear. It's the old you screaming as it's being burned away. It's terrifying to witness, let alone experience.

You might be questioning everything you ever believed. God, the universe, reality itself. It all feels flimsy, like a thin veil that's been torn away, revealing something vast and terrifying underneath. You feel exposed, vulnerable, raw. And you just want it to stop. You'd give anything for it to stop. I know that desperation. I've felt it clawing at my throat. But here's the thing: this isn't a punishment. It's a process. A brutal, messy, often mismanaged process, but a process nonetheless. It's trying to move you somewhere. Somewhere new. Somewhere you can't even conceive of right now because you're too busy fighting for your life.

What's Happening to You

Let's talk about what might actually be going on. If you're experiencing what feels like a spiritual emergency, it's often linked to an awakening of what's called kundalini energy. It's a powerful force, often described as coiled at the base of your spine, and when it awakens, it can move up through your central nervous system, clearing out blockages, old traumas, conditioning - anything that's not serving your true self. And that clearing, man, it's violent. It's not gentle. It's like a cosmic pressure washer blasting through your entire being. That's why you feel so much chaos. It's literally reorganizing your entire system from the inside out.

You might have heard about this energy in spiritual circles, but usually, it's talked about in glowing terms, like it's all bliss and enlightenment. And sure, that's the eventual outcome for some, but the journey there? It can be hell. Absolute hell. Especially if it's an unbidden awakening, or if you've been doing practices that have stirred it up without proper guidance. You weren't prepared for this. Nobody really is, not for the full force of it. You're feeling the effects of this massive internal shift, and your body and mind are struggling to keep up. It's like your internal operating system is being completely rewritten while the computer is still running. It's gonna glitch. It's gonna crash. It's gonna throw error messages at you that make no sense.

This isn't just "stress" or "anxiety" in the conventional sense, though it mimics those things perfectly. It's deeper. It's existential. It's a spiritual crisis, and it requires a different kind of understanding and a different kind of support. You need to know that what you're going through has been documented. There are books written by people who've studied this, who've been through it themselves. I found immense comfort, even in my darkest moments, knowing that I wasn't the only one. Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link) was one of those books for me. It helped put words to the indescribable chaos within. Another one that gave me some grounding was The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link). These aren't quick fixes, but they're lifelines. They're proof that you're not a medical anomaly, but part of a known, albeit rare and intense, human experience.

Finding Your Anchor Points

Okay, so you're in the thick of it. What do you do? How do you find hope when every fiber of your being is screaming despair? You find your anchor points. These are the things, however small, that can ground you, even for a moment, in the present and away from the terrifying narratives your mind is weaving. It's not about stopping the process - you can't, not really - but about managing the symptoms and finding pockets of peace.

  • Acknowledge and Validate: First, just acknowledge what's happening. Say it out loud, even if it's just to yourself: "I'm going through a kundalini awakening/spiritual emergency, and it's terrifying." Giving it a name, even a scary one, can take away some of its amorphous power. It's real. Your pain is real. Your fear is real. Don't try to intellectualize it away or pretend it's not happening.
  • Grounding Practices: This is huge. When your energy is flying everywhere, you need to pull it back down to earth.
    • Nature: Get outside. Put your bare feet on the grass or dirt. Lean against a tree. Feel the wind on your face. Look at the sky. Connect with something bigger and more stable than your internal turmoil.
    • Your Body: Focus on your breath. Not trying to change it, just observe it. Feel your feet on the floor. Touch something real - a blanket, a pet, your own hand. Do some gentle stretches. Anything that brings you back into your physical form.
    • Food: Eat nourishing, simple food. Don't skip meals, even if you don't feel like eating. Your body is working overtime, and it needs fuel.
  • Limit Stimulation: Turn off the news. Step away from social media. Avoid loud noises, bright lights, chaotic environments. Your nervous system is already overloaded. Give it a break.
  • Seek Understanding (Carefully): You need information, but be discerning. Not everyone understands this. Talk to trusted friends or family, but don't expect them to fully grasp it. They might try to "fix" you or tell you you're imagining things. Look for resources like the books I mentioned, or communities online that focus on spiritual emergency. Just be cautious of anyone promising instant cures or charging exorbitant fees.

You're not looking for a "cure" right now, you're looking for stability. You're looking for moments where you can catch your breath. That's your hope. Those small moments where the terror recedes, even for a minute. Those are the proof that it won't last forever, that there are breaks in the storm. Does that land?

Managing the Fear and the Future

The fear is probably the worst part, isn't it? That relentless, bone-deep terror. It's primal. It tells you you're dying, you're going insane, you're permanently damaged. And honestly, it feels like all of those things. But here's the truth: the fear is a symptom. It's your ego's last stand. It's the old programming fighting against the new. When the fear comes, try - and I know this is hard - to not get swept away by its story. Observe it. "Oh, there's that fear again. Telling me I'm going to die. Okay. I hear you, fear." Don't engage with it. Don't feed it. Just notice it. It's like watching a scary movie - you know it's not real, even if it feels real in the moment. The fear is real, but the story it tells about your future isn't necessarily so.

You might be wondering, "How long will this last?" And honestly, I can't give you a definitive answer. It varies wildly for everyone. Some people go through intense periods for weeks or months, others for years. It's not a linear process either; you'll have good days and bad days, periods of intensity followed by lulls. That's part of the frustration, I know. But each wave, each intense period, is doing something. It's clearing something out. It's moving you forward, even if it feels like you're just drowning. You're not just enduring; you're transforming. You're being remade.

One of the hardest parts is the isolation. You feel like no one understands. And many won't. But there are people who do. Organizations like the Spiritual Emergence Network (SEN) have resources and can connect you with people who understand what you're going through. Reading accounts from others, like those in When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) or The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link), can be incredibly validating. You're not alone in this dark place, even if it feels that way at 3 AM. Millions of people have gone through this, are going through this, and will go through this. You're part of a vast, often hidden, human experience.

Holding Onto Hope

So, how do you hold onto hope when everything feels hopeless? You remember that this is temporary. This intense phase, these terrifying symptoms - they are temporary. They will shift. They will change. You won't feel this way forever. You can't. Your system simply can't sustain this level of intensity indefinitely. It will eventually integrate, calm down, and find a new equilibrium. It might not be the equilibrium you had before, but it will be an equilibrium.

Hope isn't about pretending everything is fine. It's about knowing that even in the deepest darkness, there's a flicker of light, a possibility of change. It's about trusting, even when you can't see the path, that there is a path. It's about recognizing that this process, as brutal as it is, is ultimately for your growth. It's stripping away all the false layers, all the things that aren't truly you, so that your authentic self can emerge. And that self, let me tell you, it's something truly incredible.

You're stronger than you think. You're more resilient than you can possibly imagine right now. You're enduring something that most people can't even conceive of, and you're still here. You're still fighting. That's hope right there. That's the spark. Keep breathing. Keep grounding. Keep seeking out those small moments of peace. Don't give up. You're going to get through this. You really are. It won't be easy, but you'll come out the other side, changed, yes, but also more real, more alive, and more deeply connected than you ever thought possible. Hold on. Just hold on.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
